title: Safe Enteral Nutrition via a New PEG (Gastrostomy) Tube in a 72-Year-Old Man with Severe Oropharyngeal Dysphagia After a Recent Ischemic Stroke: Confirming Tube Placement (NOT by Auscultation), ASPIRATION PREVENTION (Head-of-Bed Elevation + Tolerance Monitoring), Maintaining Tube Patency with Water Flushes, Correct Medication Administration Through the Tube (Liquids; Never Crushing Extended-Release/Enteric-Coated), Free-Water Hydration, Peristomal Skin and Complication Surveillance, and Patient/Family Self-Management Teaching
subtitle: ENTERAL NUTRITION (tube feeding) delivers nutrition directly into a functioning GI tract when a patient cannot safely meet needs by mouth — here, severe oropharyngeal DYSPHAGIA after a stroke (a failed bedside/instrumented swallow evaluation, high ASPIRATION risk). 'If the gut works, use it': enteral feeding is preferred over parenteral when the GI tract is functional. Access route depends on duration: a NASOGASTRIC/nasoenteric tube is used SHORT-term (generally < 4-6 weeks), while a PERCUTANEOUS ENDOSCOPIC GASTROSTOMY (PEG) is placed for LONG-term feeding. PLACEMENT VERIFICATION is a core safety step: after a NEW tube is placed, RADIOGRAPHIC (X-ray) confirmation is the GOLD STANDARD and must occur BEFORE the first use; for ongoing checks the nurse verifies the EXTERNAL tube length/markings (for migration), aspirates gastric contents and checks pH (a pH ≤ 5 supports gastric placement), and assesses the exit-site/external bumper — AUSCULTATION of an insufflated air bolus (the 'whoosh' over the stomach) is UNRELIABLE and must NOT be used to confirm placement. ASPIRATION PREVENTION is the single most important ongoing concern: keep the HEAD OF BED ELEVATED to AT LEAST 30-45° during feeding and for 30-60 minutes afterward (and continuously for continuous feeds); confirm placement before intermittent feeds/medications; assess tolerance; and watch for aspiration (coughing, new respiratory distress, falling SpO2, crackles, fever). TOLERANCE/RESIDUALS: monitor for abdominal distension, nausea/vomiting, and — per facility policy — gastric residual volume; when residuals are checked, RETURN the aspirated contents (to avoid fluid/electrolyte loss) and hold/reassess only for high volumes or other signs of intolerance; do NOT routinely discard gastric contents. TUBE PATENCY: FLUSH with ~30 mL of WATER before and after each feeding and each medication, between medications, and every 4-6 hours during continuous feeds — water (not juice or soda, which can curdle/clog); for a clog, attempt warm water and gentle pressure per policy. FORMULA/INFECTION CONTROL: use clean technique, observe hang-time limits, prefer closed systems, label and refrigerate opened formula, and do not 'top off' old formula. MEDICATION ADMINISTRATION through the tube: use LIQUID formulations whenever available; if a tablet must be crushed, crush ONLY immediate-release tablets approved for crushing and dissolve in water — NEVER crush ENTERIC-COATED or EXTENDED/SUSTAINED-RELEASE products (loss of the delivery mechanism → toxicity/clogging); give each medication SEPARATELY and FLUSH between them; do NOT add medications to the feeding formula; and hold tube feeds around interacting drugs (classically PHENYTOIN) per policy. HYDRATION: provide scheduled FREE-WATER flushes (enteral formulas do not supply enough free water) and monitor intake/output and electrolytes. COMPLICATIONS to recognize and prevent: ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA (most serious), DIARRHEA (too-rapid rate, hyperosmolar formula, sorbitol-containing meds, contamination/C. difficile), constipation, DEHYDRATION (inadequate free water), REFEEDING SYNDROME in malnourished patients (start low, monitor phosphate/potassium/magnesium), hyperglycemia, tube CLOGGING or DISLODGEMENT/migration, and peristomal skin breakdown/leakage/infection or a buried bumper. A DISLODGED PEG is urgent — especially a FRESH/IMMATURE tract (< ~4 weeks): notify the provider immediately and do NOT blindly reinsert it (the tract can close quickly and blind reinsertion risks a false passage). COMFORT/PSYCHOSOCIAL: provide ORAL CARE even when NPO, support the patient through the loss of the pleasure/social role of eating and any body-image concerns, and involve speech-language pathology (ongoing swallow re-evaluation) and a dietitian. TEACHING for self-management/discharge: the patient and family demonstrate (teach-back) head-of-bed elevation, placement/patency checks and flushing, feeding and medication technique, free-water schedule, skin care, and the warning signs to report (coughing/choking, respiratory distress, fever, abdominal pain/distension, persistent diarrhea or no output, tube dislodgement, or signs of dehydration). NURSING ROLE: deliver enteral nutrition safely — verify placement correctly, prevent aspiration with head-of-bed elevation and tolerance monitoring, maintain tube patency and skin integrity, administer medications correctly, ensure hydration, recognize and prevent complications, support comfort and dignity, and teach the patient/family for safe long-term self-management.

patient.name: H. Tran
patient.code: Full code; med-surg unit; severe oropharyngeal dysphagia after a recent ischemic stroke, with a newly placed PEG (gastrostomy) tube; goal = safe enteral nutrition with aspiration prevention, correct tube/feeding/medication management, hydration, complication recognition, and patient/family self-management teaching for discharge
patient.admitDate: Recovering on the medical-surgical unit after an ischemic stroke ~3 weeks ago that left him with severe oropharyngeal DYSPHAGIA; a bedside and instrumented swallow evaluation confirmed he cannot safely take food/liquids by mouth (high aspiration risk), so a PERCUTANEOUS ENDOSCOPIC GASTROSTOMY (PEG) tube was placed for long-term enteral nutrition. X-ray confirmed gastric placement before the first use. He is alert and able to participate; his daughter will help with care at home. The team is establishing safe enteral feeding and beginning comprehensive self-management teaching (placement/patency checks, aspiration prevention, feeding and medication technique, hydration, skin care, and complication recognition).
patient.room: Medical-surgical unit; interdisciplinary team (medical provider, med-surg RN, registered dietitian, speech-language pathology for ongoing swallow re-evaluation, pharmacy, case management for home enteral therapy); enteral feeding pump available; PEG site intact

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; before feeding the nurse elevates the HEAD OF BED to 30-45°, confirms PEG patency and external-marking position (no migration), and verifies that radiographic placement confirmation was obtained before first use; alert, severe dysphagia (failed swallow eval) → high aspiration risk

37.0°C; abdomen soft and non-distended, bowel sounds present, no nausea; gastric residual checked per policy is within limits and RETURNED (not discarded); feeds tolerated with the head of bed kept elevated

37.0°C; LIQUID medications used where available; the nurse flushes ~30 mL water before and after and BETWEEN each medication and gives them separately; extended-release/enteric-coated tablets are NOT crushed (immediate-release/liquid substitutes ordered); tube feeds held around the interacting drug (phenytoin) per policy

37.0°C; scheduled FREE-WATER flushes given for hydration (formula alone is insufficient), routine flushes maintain patency, peristomal skin is clean/dry/intact with the external bumper correctly positioned, and oral care is provided despite NPO status

labs.v: 72 y/o man H. Tran, ~3 weeks after an ischemic stroke, with severe oropharyngeal DYSPHAGIA confirmed unsafe for oral intake (failed swallow evaluation, high aspiration risk). ENTERAL NUTRITION is indicated because he cannot safely meet needs by mouth but has a FUNCTIONING GI tract ('if the gut works, use it' — enteral is preferred over parenteral when the GI tract works). Access route follows duration: NG/nasoenteric for SHORT-term (< 4-6 weeks); a PEG (gastrostomy) for LONG-term feeding — so a PEG was placed. PLACEMENT VERIFICATION: after a NEW tube is placed, X-RAY confirmation is the GOLD STANDARD and must be done BEFORE the first use (done here); for ongoing checks the nurse confirms the EXTERNAL tube length/markings (migration), aspirates gastric contents and checks pH (≤5 supports gastric placement), and inspects the exit site/external bumper. AUSCULTATION of an air bolus ('whoosh') is UNRELIABLE and is NOT used to confirm placement. PMH: ischemic stroke with residual dysphagia; alert and able to participate; daughter will assist at home. The nursing focus is safe enteral feeding (aspiration prevention, tolerance, patency, correct medications, hydration, skin/complication surveillance) and comprehensive self-management teaching.
labs.r: Enteral nutrition for a patient who can't safely eat but has a WORKING gut (enteral preferred over parenteral). Route by duration: NG short-term (<4-6 wks); PEG long-term. PLACEMENT: NEW tube → X-RAY before first use (gold standard); ongoing → external markings + gastric pH (≤5) + site check; AUSCULTATION is UNRELIABLE (don't use). RN role: confirm placement correctly + set up safe feeding + teach.

labs.v: ASPIRATION is the most important ongoing risk of tube feeding (especially with dysphagia). PREVENTION: keep the HEAD OF BED ELEVATED to AT LEAST 30-45° DURING the feeding and for 30-60 minutes AFTER (continuous for continuous feeds); confirm placement before intermittent feeds/medications; and watch for aspiration signs — coughing/choking, new respiratory distress, falling SpO2, crackles, fever. A bolus that is too rapid or a high gastric residual increases risk; if the patient coughs or shows distress, STOP/slow the feed, keep the head of bed up, and reassess. TOLERANCE: assess the abdomen (distension, pain), nausea/vomiting, bowel function, and — per facility policy — gastric residual volume. When residuals are checked, RETURN the aspirated contents (discarding them wastes fluid/electrolytes/acid) and HOLD/reassess only for high volumes (per protocol) or other intolerance; current guidance de-emphasizes routine GRV but many facilities still monitor it. Signs of INTOLERANCE: abdominal distension/pain, nausea/vomiting, high residuals, or diarrhea → slow or hold the feed and notify the provider. Continuous pump feeding (vs bolus) may be better tolerated in high-aspiration-risk patients.
labs.r: Aspiration = the key risk. PREVENT: HOB ≥30-45° during + 30-60 min after (continuous for continuous feeds); confirm placement before feeds/meds; watch for cough/distress/desat/crackles/fever → STOP/slow + keep HOB up + reassess. TOLERANCE: abdomen/nausea/residuals per policy; RETURN aspirated residuals (don't discard); hold for high residuals/intolerance (distension, vomiting, diarrhea). Continuous feeds may be better tolerated in high-risk patients. RN role: aspiration prevention + tolerance monitoring.

labs.n: Tube patency (flushing) + medication administration + formula/infection control
labs.v: PATENCY: FLUSH with ~30 mL of WATER before and after each feeding and each medication, BETWEEN medications, and every 4-6 hours during continuous feeds — use WATER, not juice/soda (these can curdle/clog the tube). For a clog, attempt warm water with gentle pressure/aspiration per policy (avoid forcing or inserting objects). MEDICATION ADMINISTRATION through the tube is high-yield and error-prone: use LIQUID formulations whenever available; if a tablet must be crushed, crush ONLY immediate-release tablets that are approved for crushing and dissolve in water — NEVER crush ENTERIC-COATED or EXTENDED/SUSTAINED-RELEASE tablets/capsules (crushing destroys the delivery mechanism, risking toxicity and clogging). Give each medication SEPARATELY and FLUSH between them; do NOT add medications to the feeding formula; and HOLD tube feeds before/after interacting drugs (classically PHENYTOIN, which binds enteral formula) per pharmacy/policy. FORMULA/INFECTION CONTROL: use clean technique and hand hygiene, observe HANG-TIME limits, prefer CLOSED systems, label with date/time, refrigerate opened formula, and do NOT add fresh formula to old ('topping off') — contamination causes diarrhea/infection.
labs.r: PATENCY: flush ~30 mL WATER before/after feeds + before/after AND between meds + q4-6h on continuous; water not juice/soda. MEDS: liquids preferred; crush ONLY crushable immediate-release; NEVER crush enteric-coated/extended-release; give each med separately + flush between; don't add meds to formula; hold feeds around phenytoin. FORMULA: clean technique, hang-time limits, closed system, refrigerate opened, don't 'top off.' RN role: keep tube patent + safe med admin + safe formula handling.

labs.n: Complications, hydration, skin/psychosocial + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: COMPLICATIONS to recognize/prevent: ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA (most serious — prevented by head-of-bed elevation, placement checks, tolerance monitoring); DIARRHEA (too-rapid rate, hyperosmolar formula, sorbitol-containing liquid meds, or contamination/C. difficile — slow the rate, review meds/formula, evaluate infection); CONSTIPATION; DEHYDRATION (enteral formula alone provides insufficient free water → give scheduled FREE-WATER flushes and monitor I&O/electrolytes/BUN); REFEEDING SYNDROME in malnourished patients (start low/advance slowly, monitor and replete PHOSPHATE/POTASSIUM/MAGNESIUM); HYPERGLYCEMIA (monitor glucose); tube CLOGGING and DISLODGEMENT/migration; and PERISTOMAL skin breakdown/leakage/infection or a buried bumper (assess and protect the skin). A DISLODGED PEG is urgent — for a FRESH/immature tract (< ~4 weeks) NOTIFY THE PROVIDER IMMEDIATELY and do NOT blindly reinsert (the tract can close quickly and blind reinsertion can create a false passage). COMFORT/PSYCHOSOCIAL: provide ORAL CARE even when NPO, address the loss of the pleasure and social role of eating and body-image concerns, and involve speech-language pathology (ongoing swallow re-evaluation, possible future oral trials) and a dietitian. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT lay the patient flat to feed; did NOT confirm placement by auscultation alone; did NOT crush an extended-release tablet or add medications to the formula; did NOT flush with juice/soda or skip flushes; did NOT withhold free water; and did NOT blindly reinsert a freshly dislodged PEG. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: correct placement verification; head-of-bed elevation + tolerance monitoring; routine water flushes; correct medication technique; adequate free-water hydration; skin care; complication surveillance; oral care/psychosocial support; and thorough teach-back education with dietitian/speech follow-up. EXPECTED outcome: feeds are tolerated without aspiration, nutrition and hydration improve, the tube stays patent and the skin intact, and the patient/family safely self-manage at home.
labs.r: Complications: ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA (HOB up + checks), DIARRHEA (rate/osmolality/sorbitol meds/contamination/C. diff), constipation, DEHYDRATION (free-water flushes + I&O), REFEEDING (start low, monitor phos/K/Mg), hyperglycemia, clog/dislodgement, peristomal skin/buried bumper. DISLODGED fresh PEG (<~4 wks) → notify provider, DON'T blindly reinsert. Oral care even if NPO + psychosocial + SLP/dietitian. Negatives confirm correct practice (not flat, not auscultation-only, no crushing ER/adding to formula, no juice flushes, free water given, no blind reinsertion). RN role: prevent/recognize complications + hydrate + skin care + teach.

labs.v: 2.4 mg/dL (low — monitor for refeeding syndrome as enteral nutrition advances; replete and advance feeds slowly)
3.6 mEq/L (low-normal; monitor with refeeding alongside phosphate and magnesium)
142 mg/dL (mild hyperglycemia — expected to monitor with continuous enteral feeds)
12 mg/dL (low — reflects baseline undernutrition; track response to enteral nutrition with the dietitian)

nursesNote: Med-surg — 72 y/o man, ~3 weeks post ischemic stroke with severe oropharyngeal DYSPHAGIA (failed swallow eval, high aspiration risk); new PEG placed for long-term enteral nutrition, X-ray-confirmed before first use. Enteral preferred because the gut works. ROUTE by duration (NG <4-6 wks; PEG long-term). PLACEMENT checks: external markings/length (migration), gastric pH (≤5), site/bumper — AUSCULTATION is UNRELIABLE, not used. ASPIRATION PREVENTION (key risk): HOB ≥30-45° during feeds + 30-60 min after; confirm placement before intermittent feeds/meds; watch for cough/choking/distress/desat/crackles/fever → STOP/slow + keep HOB up + reassess. TOLERANCE: soft non-distended abdomen, BS present, no nausea; residual per policy within limits and RETURNED (not discarded); slow/hold for distension/vomiting/diarrhea/high residual. PATENCY: flush ~30 mL WATER before/after feeds + before/after AND between meds + q4-6h continuous (water, not juice/soda). MEDS: liquids preferred; crush only crushable immediate-release; NEVER crush enteric-coated/extended-release; give separately + flush between; don't add to formula; hold feeds around phenytoin per policy. FORMULA: clean technique, hang-time limits, closed system, refrigerate opened, don't 'top off.' HYDRATION: scheduled FREE-WATER flushes (formula insufficient) + monitor I&O/lytes. COMPLICATIONS watched: aspiration pneumonia, diarrhea (rate/osmolality/sorbitol meds/contamination/C. diff), constipation, dehydration, REFEEDING (phos 2.4 low, K 3.6, Mg — start low/advance slow/replete), hyperglycemia (glucose 142), clog/dislodgement, peristomal skin/buried bumper; prealbumin 12 (baseline undernutrition). DISLODGED fresh PEG (<~4 wks) → notify provider, DON'T blindly reinsert. Oral care given despite NPO; psychosocial support for loss of eating; SLP (swallow re-eval) + dietitian involved. During one bolus a brief cough → slowed feed, HOB up, lungs clear, SpO2 recovered. TEACHING (teach-back with daughter): HOB elevation, placement/patency checks + flushing, feeding/med technique, free-water schedule, skin care, and warning signs (cough/choking, respiratory distress, fever, abdominal pain/distension, persistent diarrhea/no output, tube dislodgement, dehydration). NEGATIVES (correct practice): not fed flat, not auscultation-only, no crushing ER/adding meds to formula, no juice flushes, free water given, no blind reinsertion. Outcome: tolerating goal feeds, nutrition/hydration improving, skin intact, no aspiration, family demonstrates safe technique.
providerOrders: DIET/NUTRITION: enteral nutrition via PEG; dietitian to set formula, goal rate/volume, and free-water flush schedule; advance per tolerance; NPO by mouth (severe dysphagia)

PLACEMENT VERIFICATION: radiographic confirmation obtained before first use; nurse to verify external tube length/markings, gastric pH, and exit-site/bumper each shift and before intermittent feeds/medications; do NOT use auscultation to confirm placement

ASPIRATION PRECAUTIONS: keep head of bed elevated at least 30-45 degrees during feeding and for 30-60 minutes after (continuously for continuous feeds); monitor for coughing/respiratory distress/desaturation/crackles/fever; stop/slow the feed and keep the head of bed elevated if intolerance or aspiration is suspected

TOLERANCE MONITORING: assess abdomen, nausea/vomiting, bowel function, and gastric residual per facility policy; return aspirated residuals; hold and notify the provider for high residuals or signs of intolerance

TUBE PATENCY: flush with ~30 mL water before and after each feeding and each medication, between medications, and every 4-6 hours during continuous feeds; water only (not juice/soda); manage clogs with warm water per policy

MEDICATIONS via tube: use liquid formulations when available; crush only crushable immediate-release tablets; do NOT crush enteric-coated or extended/sustained-release products; give each medication separately and flush between; do not add medications to the formula; hold feeds before/after phenytoin per pharmacy guidance
HYDRATION: scheduled free-water flushes for hydration; strict intake/output; monitor electrolytes, BUN/creatinine, and glucose
REFEEDING PRECAUTIONS: advance feeds gradually in this undernourished patient; monitor and replete phosphate, potassium, and magnesium

PEG SITE CARE: assess and protect peristomal skin; verify external bumper position; report leakage, redness, breakdown, or a buried bumper; for a dislodged tube (fresh tract), notify the provider immediately and do NOT blindly reinsert
COMFORT/PSYCHOSOCIAL: provide oral care despite NPO; support coping with the loss of oral intake; speech-language pathology for ongoing swallow re-evaluation; dietitian follow-up

EDUCATION/DISCHARGE: teach the patient and family (teach-back) head-of-bed elevation, placement/patency checks and flushing, feeding and medication technique, the free-water schedule, skin care, and warning signs to report; case management to arrange home enteral therapy and follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for safe enteral feeding in this patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Severe dysphagia with a failed swallow evaluation makes him high-risk for aspiration — head-of-bed elevation and tolerance monitoring are essential
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — aspiration is the key risk of tube feeding, especially with dysphagia. RN role: aspiration prevention.

opts.text: Tube placement must be verified — X-ray confirmed the new PEG before first use, and ongoing checks use external markings and gastric pH (not auscultation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct placement verification prevents instilling feed/medication into a misplaced tube. RN role: verify placement correctly.

opts.text: Tolerance signs — abdominal distension, nausea/vomiting, bowel function, and residuals per policy — guide whether to continue, slow, or hold feeds
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — monitoring tolerance prevents distension/aspiration and detects intolerance. RN role: assess tolerance.

opts.text: The tube must be flushed with water to stay patent, and medications must be given correctly (liquids; never crushing extended-release/enteric-coated)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — flushing prevents clogs and correct medication technique prevents toxicity/clogging. RN role: patency + safe med admin.

opts.text: Enteral formula provides insufficient free water — scheduled free-water flushes and intake/output monitoring are needed for hydration
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — dehydration is a common, preventable complication. RN role: ensure free-water hydration.

opts.text: He is undernourished (low prealbumin/phosphate) — advance feeds gradually and monitor for refeeding syndrome; watch glucose and the peristomal skin
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refeeding risk, glycemic monitoring, and skin integrity all matter. RN role: refeeding precautions + skin/glucose surveillance.

opts.text: Lay the patient flat during feeding to make administration easier
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Feeding flat sharply increases aspiration risk; keep the head of bed elevated at least 30-45° during and after feeding.

opts.text: Confirm tube placement simply by injecting air and auscultating a 'whoosh' over the stomach before each feeding
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Auscultation is unreliable and is not an acceptable method to confirm placement; use external markings/length, gastric pH, and X-ray for new tubes.

opts.text: Enteral feeding is preferred when the GI tract works; route depends on duration (NG short-term, PEG long-term)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the indication and route principle. Foundation for the plan.

opts.text: X-ray is the gold standard to confirm a newly placed tube before first use; ongoing verification uses external markings and gastric pH, not auscultation
opts.rat: CORRECT — placement verification. Foundation for safe instillation.

opts.text: Keeping the head of bed elevated at least 30-45° during and after feeding is a primary aspiration-prevention measure
opts.rat: CORRECT — head-of-bed elevation. Foundation for aspiration prevention.

opts.text: Flushing with water keeps the tube patent; medications are given as liquids when possible, separately, with flushes between — never crushing extended-release/enteric-coated forms
opts.rat: CORRECT — patency and medication principles. Foundation for safe administration.

opts.text: Free-water flushes are needed for hydration, and a malnourished patient must be advanced slowly with monitoring for refeeding syndrome
opts.rat: CORRECT — hydration and refeeding principles. Foundation for fluid/electrolyte safety.

opts.text: When gastric residual is checked per policy, the aspirated contents are returned (not discarded) to avoid fluid/electrolyte loss
opts.rat: CORRECT — residual handling. Foundation for tolerance monitoring without causing losses.

opts.text: Medications can simply be added to the feeding formula bag to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Medications are never added to the formula (incompatibilities, clogging, inaccurate dosing); give them separately and flush between.

opts.text: If the tube clogs, flushing with cranberry juice or soda is the best way to dissolve the clog
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Juice/soda can worsen clogging; use warm water with gentle pressure per policy, and flush routinely with water to prevent clogs.

opts.text: Confirm correct tube placement and elevate the head of bed to at least 30-45° before instilling any feeding or medication
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Verifying placement and elevating the head of bed prevent misplaced instillation and aspiration — the most dangerous risks. RN role: placement + positioning first.

opts.text: Assess feeding tolerance (abdomen, nausea, residual per policy) and slow/hold for intolerance
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Tolerance assessment prevents distension/aspiration and catches problems early. RN role: monitor tolerance.

opts.text: Administer the feeding and medications correctly (right rate; liquids/flushes; never crushing extended-release/enteric-coated)
opts.rat: #3 — Delivers nutrition and medications safely once placement/positioning/tolerance are addressed. RN role: correct administration.

opts.text: Maintain hydration with free-water flushes and keep the tube patent and the peristomal skin intact
opts.rat: #4 — Sustains hydration, tube function, and skin integrity. RN role: hydration + patency + skin care.

opts.text: Provide oral care and psychosocial support and teach the patient/family for safe self-management
opts.rat: #5 — Comfort, dignity, and education sustain safe long-term care. RN role: comfort + teaching.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for safe enteral feeding.

opts.text: Keep the head of bed elevated at least 30-45° during feeding and for 30-60 minutes afterward
opts.rat: Primary aspiration-prevention measure. RN role: positioning.

opts.text: Verify placement using external markings and gastric pH (with X-ray for the new tube), not auscultation
opts.rat: Correct, reliable verification before instilling anything. RN role: placement check.

opts.text: Flush with ~30 mL of water before and after feedings and medications and between medications
opts.rat: Maintains patency and prevents clogging. RN role: routine water flushes.

opts.text: Give medications as liquids when available, separately, flushing between — and never crush extended-release or enteric-coated forms
opts.rat: Prevents toxicity, clogging, and incompatibility. RN role: safe medication administration.

opts.text: Provide scheduled free-water flushes for hydration and monitor intake/output and electrolytes
opts.rat: Formula alone is insufficient free water; prevents dehydration. RN role: hydration.

opts.text: Advance feeds gradually with refeeding precautions, monitor glucose, and protect the peristomal skin; provide oral care and teach the family
opts.rat: Addresses refeeding, glycemia, skin, comfort, and self-management. RN role: complication prevention + teaching.

opts.text: Return gastric residual contents after checking them per policy
opts.rat: Avoids fluid/electrolyte/acid loss. RN role: correct residual handling.

opts.text: Lay the patient flat during and after feeding to ease administration
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — feeding flat markedly increases aspiration risk; keep the head of bed elevated.

opts.text: Crush an extended-release tablet and add it, with the other medications, into the feeding formula bag
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — never crush extended-release/enteric-coated forms or add medications to the formula; give liquids separately with flushes.

opts.text: Blindly reinsert a freshly dislodged PEG and resume feeding
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a fresh tract can close and blind reinsertion risks a false passage; notify the provider and do not reinsert or feed until placement is confirmed.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify tube placement and elevate the head of bed to at least 30-45° before instilling anything
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Placement verification and positioning prevent the most dangerous errors before any feed/medication. RN role: placement + positioning.

opts.text: Assess tolerance (abdomen, nausea, residual per policy) and proceed only if tolerated
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Confirming tolerance avoids distension/aspiration. RN role: tolerance check.

opts.text: Administer the feeding at the correct rate and give medications correctly (liquids, separately, flushing between)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver nutrition and medications safely. RN role: correct administration.

opts.text: Flush with water to maintain patency, give free-water flushes for hydration, and care for the peristomal skin
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Sustains patency, hydration, and skin integrity. RN role: flush + hydrate + skin care.

opts.text: Provide oral care, support coping, and teach the patient/family with teach-back
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Comfort and education enable safe self-management. RN role: comfort + teaching.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: With the head of bed elevated, confirmed placement, and tolerance monitoring, he tolerates goal feeds with no coughing, distress, or desaturation
opts.rat: Safe feeding without aspiration is the desired outcome. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Routine water flushes keep the tube patent, scheduled free-water flushes maintain hydration, and the peristomal skin stays intact
opts.rat: Patency, hydration, and skin integrity reflect good management. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Feeds are advanced gradually with normal phosphate/potassium/magnesium, glucose is controlled, and nutrition markers (prealbumin/weight) improve
opts.rat: Refeeding avoided and nutrition restored. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: The patient and daughter correctly demonstrate head-of-bed elevation, flushing, feeding/medication technique, and warning signs
opts.rat: Successful teach-back supports safe home self-management. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the patient is fed lying flat without placement confirmation; he coughs, desaturates, and develops crackles and a fever (aspiration pneumonia)
opts.rat: ⚠️ Feeding flat without verification caused aspiration. Demonstrates why head-of-bed elevation and placement checks are essential.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — an extended-release tablet is crushed into the formula and flushes are skipped; the tube clogs and he develops medication toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ Crushing extended-release forms and skipping flushes caused clogging and toxicity. Demonstrates correct medication technique and flushing.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — no free-water flushes are given and intake/output is not tracked; he becomes dehydrated with rising sodium and BUN
opts.rat: ⚠️ Inadequate free water caused dehydration. Demonstrates the need for scheduled free-water flushes and monitoring.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a freshly dislodged PEG is blindly reinserted and feeding resumed; formula enters a false tract into the peritoneum
opts.rat: ⚠️ Blind reinsertion of a fresh PEG created a false passage. Demonstrates notifying the provider and not reinserting/feeding until confirmed.

opts.text: Early in therapy, before full nutritional repletion, his weight is stable while he tolerates feeds and advances toward goal
opts.rat: Nutritional repletion takes time; a stable short-term weight while tolerating and advancing feeds is expected. Continue and reassess.

opts.text: At follow-up he is at goal feeds with adequate hydration and intact skin, and speech therapy continues to reassess for any safe future oral intake
opts.rat: Sustained safe enteral nutrition with ongoing swallow reassessment. Ideal longer-term outcome.
